languageFamilies.name: Niger-Congo
languageFamilies.geographicSpread: Sub-Saharan Africa from Senegal to South Africa
languageFamilies.description: The world's largest language family by number of languages and third-largest by speakers. It spans nearly all of Sub-Saharan Africa and includes the vast Bantu subfamily.
languageFamilies.overview: Niger-Congo is the mother of all Bantu languages, which themselves account for over 500 languages. From the Atlantic coast of Senegal to the southern tip of Africa, Niger-Congo languages unite hundreds of millions of people. The family includes tonal languages, complex noun class systems, and some of the most widely spoken African languages including Swahili, Yoruba, Igbo, and Zulu.

characteristics.name: Noun Class System
characteristics.description: Most Niger-Congo languages use noun classes (similar to grammatical gender but more complex), with prefixes indicating the class of a noun. Bantu languages can have 15-20 noun classes.

characteristics.name: Tonal Languages
characteristics.description: Pitch differences distinguish word meanings. Yoruba has 3 tones, while some languages have up to 5 distinct tones.

characteristics.name: Agglutinative Morphology
characteristics.description: Words are built by adding prefixes and suffixes. A single verb can contain information about tense, subject, object, and mood.

characteristics.name: Serial Verb Constructions
characteristics.description: Multiple verbs string together without conjunctions to describe complex actions, common in Kwa and Voltaic languages.

writingSystem.historical: N'Ko script (invented 1949 for Manding), Vai syllabary (1830s), Nsibidi symbols (Nigeria)
writingSystem.modern: Primarily Latin alphabet with tone marks. Some communities are reviving indigenous scripts.

timeline.event: Proto-Bantu emerges in Cameroon-Nigeria border region

timeline.significance: Completion of one of history's largest migrations

timeline.event: Swahili becomes East African trade language

timeline.significance: First indigenous African script in modern era

timeline.event: N'Ko script invented for Manding languages

subFamilies.name: Bantu
subFamilies.description: Largest subfamily, spanning from Cameroon to South Africa

subFamilies.name: Atlantic
subFamilies.description: West African coastal languages from Senegal to Liberia

subFamilies.name: Mande
subFamilies.description: Languages of ancient Mali and Songhai empires

subFamilies.name: Kwa
subFamilies.description: Coastal West African languages of Ghana, Togo, Benin

subFamilies.name: Volta-Niger
subFamilies.description: Major Nigerian and Beninese languages

subFamilies.name: Gur (Voltaic)
subFamilies.description: Savanna languages of Burkina Faso, Ghana, Togo

distantCousins.relationship: Possible ancient connection debated by linguists
Geographic contact led to click adoption in Nguni languages

uniqueFeatures: Noun classes using prefixes (mu-, ba-, ki-, etc.)
Verb extensions for causative, applicative, passive

historicalInfluence.diasporaInfluence: Niger-Congo languages influenced Gullah, Haitian Creole, Brazilian Portuguese, Caribbean English

languageFamilies.name: Afroasiatic
languageFamilies.geographicSpread: North Africa, Horn of Africa, Sahel, Middle East
languageFamilies.description: One of the oldest and most geographically widespread language families, including Arabic, Amharic, Somali, and Hausa. It bridges Africa and the Middle East.
languageFamilies.overview: Afroasiatic languages have shaped world history through the development of writing (Ancient Egyptian, Phoenician → Greek → Latin), monotheistic religions (Hebrew, Arabic), and major civilizations. In Africa, this family includes the Ethiopian Semitic languages with their unique Ge'ez script, the Cushitic languages of the Horn, the Berber languages of the Sahara, and the Chadic languages including Hausa.

characteristics.name: Root-and-Pattern Morphology
characteristics.description: Words are built from consonantal roots (typically 3 consonants) with vowel patterns. Arabic k-t-b → kitab (book), katib (writer), maktaba (library).

characteristics.name: Grammatical Gender
characteristics.description: Two-gender system (masculine/feminine) in most branches, affecting nouns, pronouns, verbs, and adjectives.

characteristics.name: Consonant Inventory
characteristics.description: Rich in pharyngeal and emphatic consonants not found in European languages. Ethiopian languages have ejective sounds.

characteristics.name: VSO/SVO Word Order
characteristics.description: Semitic languages often place verbs first, while others use Subject-Verb-Object like English.

writingSystem.historical: Ancient Egyptian hieroglyphs (3200 BCE), Phoenician alphabet → all Western alphabets, Ge'ez script (Ethiopia)
writingSystem.modern: Arabic script (most widespread), Ge'ez/Ethiopic script (Ethiopia, Eritrea), Latin alphabet (Somali, Hausa), Hebrew script, Tifinagh (Berber)

timeline.event: Proto-Afroasiatic spoken (possibly in Sahara or Horn of Africa)
timeline.significance: Family origin during green Sahara period

timeline.event: Egyptian branch develops along the Nile

timeline.significance: One of world's first writing systems
Ancestor of Greek, Latin, Arabic scripts

timeline.event: Arabic spreads with Islam across North Africa

subFamilies.name: Semitic
subFamilies.description: Includes Arabic, Hebrew, Amharic, Tigrinya - languages of major world religions

subFamilies.name: Cushitic
subFamilies.description: Horn of Africa's indigenous languages - Oromo, Somali, Afar

subFamilies.name: Berber
subFamilies.description: Indigenous North African languages with ancient Tifinagh script

subFamilies.name: Chadic
subFamilies.description: Centered on Hausa, the Sahel's major trade language

subFamilies.name: Egyptian
subFamilies.description: Ancient Egyptian, ancestor of Coptic (now liturgical only)

subFamilies.name: Omotic
subFamilies.description: Ethiopian languages sometimes classified separately

distantCousins.sharedFeatures: Some pronoun and number similarities proposed

historicalInfluence.diasporaInfluence: Ethiopian communities worldwide, Arabic cultural sphere, Hausa trade diaspora across Sahel

languageFamilies.name: Nilo-Saharan
languageFamilies.geographicSpread: Nile Valley, Sudan, South Sudan, East Africa, Sahara edges
languageFamilies.description: A diverse family of East African languages spoken by pastoralists and farmers from Egypt to Tanzania, including the famous Maasai, Dinka, and Luo peoples.
languageFamilies.overview: Nilo-Saharan languages are spoken by some of Africa's most iconic peoples - the tall Dinka and Nuer of South Sudan, the cattle-herding Maasai, the marathon-running Kalenjin, and the fishing Luo. These are the languages of the Nile Valley's ancient kingdoms and East Africa's cattle cultures. The family includes the Nilotic languages, associated with striking physical characteristics and pastoral traditions.

characteristics.name: Vowel Harmony
characteristics.description: Vowels in a word must be from the same set (ATR harmony). Common in Nilotic languages.

characteristics.name: Vowel Length Distinction
characteristics.description: Short vs. long vowels change word meanings - important in Luo, Dinka, and Maasai.

characteristics.name: Complex Verb Morphology
characteristics.description: Verbs carry extensive information about aspect, mood, direction, and participants.

characteristics.name: Singulative Marking
characteristics.description: Some languages mark singular rather than plural - collective nouns are the default.

writingSystem.historical: Meroitic script (ancient Nubia, partially deciphered), Egyptian influence in Nubian kingdoms
writingSystem.modern: Latin alphabet with various diacritics, Arabic script for some Nubian languages

timeline.event: Proto-Nilo-Saharan spoken in green Sahara region
timeline.significance: Family emerges during humid African period

timeline.event: Nilotic peoples begin southward migration from Nile Valley
timeline.significance: Cattle culture spreads to East Africa

timeline.event: Nubian kingdoms develop along the Nile

timeline.significance: Pastoralists arrive in Great Rift Valley

timeline.event: Luo migration from South Sudan to Lake Victoria
Kalenjin emerge as distinct political identity

subFamilies.name: Nilotic
subFamilies.description: East African pastoralist languages - Maasai, Luo, Kalenjin, Dinka

subFamilies.name: Eastern Sudanic
subFamilies.description: Ancient Nile Valley languages including Nubian

subFamilies.name: Central Sudanic
subFamilies.description: Languages of South Sudan, CAR, and DRC borders

subFamilies.name: Saharan
subFamilies.description: Desert languages including Kanuri of the Lake Chad region

distantCousins.sharedFeatures: Some shared vocabulary in contact zones

distantCousins.relationship: Ancient Nile Valley contact with Cushitic and Egyptian

uniqueFeatures: Singulative marking (singular, not plural, is marked)
Complex case systems in some branches

historicalInfluence.diasporaInfluence: Luo political influence (Obama family heritage), Maasai cultural recognition worldwide

languageFamilies.name: Khoisan
languageFamilies.geographicSpread: Southern Africa (Namibia, Botswana, South Africa), isolated groups in Tanzania
languageFamilies.description: Among the world's oldest language families, famous for their click consonants. Spoken by the San (Bushmen) and Khoekhoe peoples - humanity's oldest genetic lineages.
languageFamilies.overview: Khoisan languages are linguistic treasures representing some of humanity's deepest linguistic heritage. The San peoples (formerly called 'Bushmen') have inhabited Southern Africa for over 100,000 years, making them among the oldest continuous populations on Earth. Their click languages influenced neighboring Bantu languages like Zulu and Xhosa, which borrowed click consonants through centuries of contact.

characteristics.name: Click Consonants
characteristics.description: Up to 100+ click consonants in some languages, made by creating suction in the mouth. Written as !, ǂ, ǀ, ǁ, ǃ in phonetic notation.

characteristics.name: Tone Systems
characteristics.description: Complex tonal systems with 4-5 tones that distinguish word meanings.

characteristics.name: Extensive Consonant Inventory
characteristics.description: Some Khoisan languages have over 140 distinct consonants - among the largest inventories known.

characteristics.name: Pharyngeal Sounds
characteristics.description: Various throat sounds beyond clicks, including uvular and pharyngeal consonants.

writingSystem.historical: Oral tradition only - no indigenous writing systems
writingSystem.modern: Latin alphabet with special symbols for clicks (!, ǂ, ǀ, ǁ, ǃ, ʘ)

timeline.event: Ancestors of Khoisan peoples inhabit Southern Africa
Rock art traditions begin in Southern Africa
Bantu peoples arrive in Southern Africa
timeline.significance: Click sounds borrowed into Nguni languages
Colonial pressure on Khoisan peoples begins

timeline.event: ǃXóõ recognized as most complex sound system

subFamilies.name: Khoe-Kwadi
subFamilies.description: Largest Khoisan group including Nama and Khoekhoe

subFamilies.name: Tuu
subFamilies.description: Southern Africa's original languages, critically endangered

subFamilies.name: Kx'a
subFamilies.description: Northern Kalahari languages

subFamilies.name: Sandawe & Hadza
subFamilies.description: Isolated click languages in Tanzania - possible Khoisan relatives

distantCousins.sharedFeatures: Zulu/Xhosa have 15-20 click sounds from Khoisan

distantCousins.relationship: Some theorize clicks were features of proto-human language

uniqueFeatures: Largest consonant inventories known to linguistics
Complex morphology with many noun classes
Extremely endangered - many have only elderly speakers

empires: No centralized states - egalitarian band societies
historicalInfluence.diasporaInfluence: San cultural tourism, /Xam folklore preserved in Bleek-Lloyd collection

languageFamilies.name: Bantu
languageFamilies.geographicSpread: Central, East, and Southern Africa - from Cameroon to South Africa
languageFamilies.description: The most widespread language subfamily in Africa, result of one of humanity's greatest migrations. From Swahili to Zulu, Bantu languages unite over a third of Africa.
languageFamilies.overview: The Bantu expansion is one of the most significant events in African history. Starting around 3000 BCE from the Cameroon-Nigeria border region, Bantu-speaking farmers spread their languages, iron-working technology, and agricultural practices across central, eastern, and southern Africa over 4,000 years. Today, Bantu languages include Swahili (100M+ speakers), Shona, Zulu, Kikuyu, Lingala, and hundreds more.

characteristics.name: Noun Class Prefixes
characteristics.description: 15-20 noun classes marked by prefixes. Swahili: m-tu (person), wa-tu (people), ki-tu (thing), vi-tu (things).

characteristics.name: Agglutinative Verbs
characteristics.description: Single words express complete sentences: Swahili 'nitakupenda' = ni-ta-ku-penda = I-will-you-love = 'I will love you'

characteristics.name: Vowel Harmony
characteristics.description: Vowels in suffixes harmonize with verb root vowels in many Bantu languages.

characteristics.name: Shared Core Vocabulary
characteristics.description: Common roots across all Bantu: *-ntu (person), *-amba (speak), *-genda (go), *-kúmà (to be heavy)

writingSystem.historical: Primarily oral tradition, though Swahili used Arabic script from ~1100 CE

timeline.event: First wave moves into Congo Basin rainforest
Eastern Bantu reach Great Lakes region
Bantu speakers reach East African coast
Great Zimbabwe civilization built by Shona speakers
Swahili becomes Indian Ocean trade language

subFamilies.name: Nguni
subFamilies.description: Southern African languages with click consonants - Zulu, Xhosa, Ndebele

subFamilies.name: Sotho-Tswana
subFamilies.description: Southern African plateau languages

subFamilies.name: Great Lakes Bantu
subFamilies.description: Languages of Uganda, Rwanda, Burundi, and western Kenya/Tanzania

subFamilies.name: Swahili & Coastal Bantu
subFamilies.description: East African coast languages, Swahili is Africa's most spoken Bantu language

subFamilies.name: Congo Basin Bantu
subFamilies.description: Rainforest and savanna languages of DRC, Angola, Congo

uniqueFeatures: Noun class prefix system (mu-/ba-, ki-/vi-, etc.)
Agglutinative verb structure encoding subject, tense, object
Click consonants in southern Nguni languages
Ubuntu philosophy expressed in shared root -ntu

historicalInfluence.diasporaInfluence: Swahili taught globally, Zulu cultural exports, Kongo influences in Caribbean

languageFamilies.name: Cushitic
languageFamilies.geographicSpread: Horn of Africa - Ethiopia, Somalia, Eritrea, Djibouti, Kenya, Sudan
languageFamilies.description: The indigenous language family of the Horn of Africa, home to Oromo (Africa's 4th most spoken language), Somali, and the ancient Beja.
languageFamilies.overview: Cushitic languages have been spoken in the Horn of Africa for over 7,000 years, making them one of the oldest continuously inhabited language areas. The Oromo are Ethiopia's largest ethnic group, while Somali unites one of Africa's most homogeneous nations. These languages carry rich pastoral traditions, complex clan genealogies, and poetry traditions that rival any in the world.

characteristics.name: SOV Word Order
characteristics.description: Subject-Object-Verb order - 'The man the cow saw' instead of 'The man saw the cow'

characteristics.name: Ejective Consonants
characteristics.description: Consonants pronounced with a burst of air from closed glottis, written as p', t', k', etc.

characteristics.name: Gender and Case System
characteristics.description: Masculine/feminine nouns with complex case marking for subject, object, location, etc.

characteristics.name: Postpositions
characteristics.description: Grammatical markers come after nouns (postpositions), not before (prepositions).

writingSystem.historical: Oral tradition primary; some Arabic script usage for religious texts
writingSystem.modern: Latin alphabet (Somali since 1972, Oromo since 1991), Ethiopic script (some Oromo)

timeline.event: Proto-Cushitic speakers in Horn of Africa
Islam reaches Somali and Afar coast

timeline.significance: Ethiopia's largest language gets standard script

subFamilies.name: Highland East Cushitic
subFamilies.description: Ethiopian highland languages including Sidama and Hadiyya

subFamilies.name: Lowland East Cushitic
subFamilies.description: Major Cushitic languages - Oromo, Somali, Afar

subFamilies.name: Central Cushitic (Agaw)
subFamilies.description: Ancient Ethiopian languages, possibly original highland inhabitants

subFamilies.name: North Cushitic
subFamilies.description: The ancient Beja language of Sudan and Eritrea

historicalInfluence.diasporaInfluence: Somali diaspora worldwide (2M+), Oromo communities in USA/Europe
